text.title: Reply Guy Protocol — Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every application of `reply-guy-protocol` must enforce. The rules pin the discipline that turns a protocol from folklore into a reviewable, owned, version-controlled operating tool.

rule.statement: Every step starts with the precondition needed (state, alert, ticket, signal). If the precondition is absent, the step is skipped, not partly executed.

rationale.source: Google SRE Workbook, chapter on runbooks; aviation checklist design

rule.rationale: Runbooks executed mid-shift fail when steps are run on the wrong system state. Preconditions are the cheap guard that prevents an emergency from becoming a second emergency.

rule.statement: Each action names the actor (role + system) and the artefact produced. 'Notify stakeholders' is rejected; 'on-call posts in #incident with template X' is accepted.
rule.rationale: Vague actors are why steps get dropped under stress. Naming both the actor and the artefact makes ownership and verification unambiguous.

rule.statement: Every action with side-effects on production carries a documented rollback or a hard 'STOP and escalate' branch. No silent failure paths.
rule.rationale: Runbooks evolve in production. A step without a rollback gets the engineer stuck mid-procedure and forces ad-hoc decisions exactly when judgment is worst.

rule.statement: The runbook publishes a wall-clock budget per phase. A phase exceeding budget triggers escalation, not 'try harder'.

rationale.source: SRE incident-management practice; ITIL change windows

rule.rationale: Without timeboxes, an early step that misfires consumes the whole window and the incident gets a second alarm. Timeboxes are how an individual operator stays inside the team's response SLO.

rule.statement: After execution, the runbook produces a structured record (start_ts, end_ts, branches taken, exceptions, artefacts) committed to the postmortem feeder folder.
rule.rationale: Runbooks without records cannot improve. The recorded run is what feeds the next refinement and the postmortem.
